source.title: Amendments to the Charter: Local Acts of the General Assembly and General Ordinances of the City of Louisville from Oct. 1, 1884, to Dec. 31, 1886 : with the Constitution of the United States of America and the Constitution of the Commonwealth of Kentucky
source.author: OLIVER LUCAS.

pages.text:
CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 69

union passenger station in said city, or may contract with said company for the use of such passenger station, and may in like manner contract with any other railway company for the use of its property in said city for terminal facilities.

§ 16. All actions or proceedings instituted by the said company for the condemnation of property for any of the uses herein mentioned shall be regulated and governed by the General Statutes relating to actions or proceedings for condemnation by writ of *ad quod damnum*.

§ 17. Said company may further acquire and hold, by gift, purchase, or condemnation, such land adjacent to its line of railway as may be necessary for the construction of machine and repair shops, and may construct and operate thereon shops and other belongings necessary to be used by it in constructing and repairing its cars and other equipments.

§ 3. This act shall take effect from and after its passage.
Approved April 19, 1886.

AN ACT TO INCORPORATE THE WEST END FERRY COMPANY.

§ 1. That Murray Keller, Ed. Hughes, L. R. McCleery, H. H. Littell, E. Bourlier and Scott Newman, their successors and assigns, are hereby created a body-politic and corporate, by the name of the "West End Ferry Company," and by that name may contract and be contracted with, sue and be sued, plead and be impleaded, answer and defend, in all courts and places as a natural person; and may have a common seal and alter the same at pleasure; and said company may have all other powers usual and incident to corporations, and may purchase and own all such property, real and personal, as may be necessary to enable said corporation to carry on the business of ferrying freight, passengers, and live stock and animals and vehicles from a point where Broadway, in city of Louisville, extended west, will strike the Ohio River, in Jefferson County, or from an accessible point within a mile on each side of Broadway, across the said Ohio River to an accessible point on the Indiana shore, and generally to do and perform all such acts as an individual might do in the premises.

CONSTITUTION OF KENTUCKY. 219

their successors be qualified; and on the first Monday in August, 1852, and on the first Monday of August in every second year thereafter, elections for sheriff shall be held: *Provided*, That the sheriffs first elected shall enter upon the duties of their respective offices on the first Monday in June, 1851, and after the first election on the first Monday in January next succeeding their election.

§ 5. A constable shall be elected in every justices' district, who shall be chosen for two years, at such time and place as may be provided by law, whose jurisdiction shall be co-extensive with the county in which he may reside.

§ 6. Officers for towns and cities shall be elected for such terms and in such manner and with such qualifications as may be prescribed by law.

§ 7. Vacancies in offices under this article shall be filled until the next regular election, in such manner as the General Assembly may provide.

§ 8. When a new county shall be erected, officers for the same, to serve until the next stated election, shall be elected or appointed in such way and at such times as the General Assembly may prescribe.

§ 9. Clerks, sheriffs, surveyors, coroners, constables, and jailers, and such other officers as the General Assembly may, from time to time, require, shall, before they enter upon the duties of their respective offices, and as often thereafter as may be deemed proper, give such bond and security as shall be prescribed by law.

§ 10. The General Assembly may provide for the election or appointment, for a term not exceeding four years, of such other county or district ministerial and executive officers as shall, from time to time, be necessary and proper.

§ 11. A county assessor shall be elected in each county at the same time, and for the same term, that the presiding judge of the county court is elected, until otherwise provided for by law. He shall have power to appoint such assistants as may be necessary and proper.

ARTICLE VII.

*Concerning the Militia.*

§ 1. The militia of this Commonwealth shall consist of all free, able-bodied male persons (negroes, mulattoes, and Indians excep-

CHARTER AMENDMENTS AND LOCAL ACTS. 75

sue and to be sued; to have and use a common seal if the directors so determine, and with power to change or alter the same at the pleasure of the directors; and with power to purchase and hold in fee or by lease real estate in the city of Louisville for a market-house and other necessary appendages and buildings in connection therewith, and with power to purchase or to do anything necessary or useful in the construction, carrying on, furnishing, and maintaining said market-house; and said corporation may, if a majority of the stockholders shall so determine, construct said market-house so that the second story may be fitted for a concert hall.

§ 2. The capital of said corporation shall not exceed one hundred and fifty thousand dollars, and shall be divided into shares of one hundred dollars each. The shares shall be personal property, and be transferable upon the books of the company; and each share shall entitle the holder to one vote at all elections for officers and at all meetings of the stockholders in connection with the business of the corporation.

§ 3. That on or before the first day of June, 1886, the stockholders shall meet and elect five directors, who shall choose one of their body president, and shall, by their by-laws, provide for annual elections of directors. The directors shall hold their office until their successors are elected and qualified, and none but stockholders shall be either president or directors of the corporation.

§ 4. The persons named in section 1, or any two of them, may act as commissioners to receive subscriptions to the stock of the company. The stockholders, or a majority of them, may at any time change the name of the corporation; and by whatever name they may assume, by that name the corporation may exercise all the corporate privileges according to the terms of this charter.

§ 5. The corporation shall have the right to borrow money on its credit to an amount not exceeding the capital stock, and to secure the same by mortgage on its property and franchises. Said corporation may, whenever deemed expedient by its directors, sell and convey such property held by it. In all such cases the president shall, for and on behalf of the corporation, and in its name, execute such deed or bill of sale, and the title of the corporation shall thereby pass to the purchaser.

§ 6. The president and directors of said corporation shall have power to divide the market-house into as many stalls as they may

CONSTITUTION OF KENTUCKY. 225

be continued in slavery by the laws of this State. They shall pass laws to permit owners of slaves to emancipate them, saving the rights of creditors, and to prevent them from remaining in this State after they are emancipated. They shall have full power to prevent slaves being brought into this State as merchandise. They shall have full power to prevent slaves being brought into this State, who have been, since the first day of January, one thousand seven hundred and eighty-nine, or may hereafter be imported into any of the United States from a foreign country. And they shall have full power to pass such laws as may be necessary to oblige the owners of slaves to treat them with humanity, to provide for them necessary clothing and provision, to abstain from all injuries to them, extending to life or limb; and in case of their neglect or refusal to comply with the directions of such laws, to have such slave or slaves sold, for the benefit of their owner or owners.

§ 2. The General Assembly shall pass laws providing that any free negro or mulatto hereafter immigrating to, and any slave hereafter emancipated in and refusing to leave this State, or having left, shall return and settle within this State, shall be deemed guilty of felony, and punished by confinement in the penitentiary thereof.

§ 3. In the prosecution of slaves for felony, no inquest by a grand jury shall be necessary, but the proceedings in such prosecutions shall be regulated by law, except that the General Assembly shall have no power to deprive them of the privilege of an impartial trial by a petit jury.

ARTICLE XI.
*Concerning Education.*

§ 1. The capital of the fund called and known as the "Common School Fund," consisting of one million two hundred and twenty-five thousand seven hundred and sixty-eight dollars and forty-two cents, for which bonds have been executed by the State to the board of education, and seventy-three thousand five hundred dollars of stock in the Bank of Kentucky; also, the sum of fifty-one thousand two hundred and twenty-three dollars and twenty-nine cents, balance of interest on the school fund for the year 1848, unexpended, together with any sum which may be hereafter raised in the State by taxation or otherwise for purposes of education, shall be held inviolate, for the purpose of sustaining a system of common schools.
